Items.Title: OpenAPI Editor - 免费的浏览器内开发工具 | OpenAPI Editor - Free IN-Browser Developer Tool
Items.Description:
你好编码员！

本文介绍了OpenAPI Editor ，这是一个免费的在线工具，开发人员可以使用它来编辑他们的 API 接口。 对于新手来说， OpenAPI是一种用于designing RESTful APIs和 Web 服务的开源格式，可作为软件开发过程中涉及的所有各方（开发人员、测试人员等）的单一事实来源。

谢谢阅读！

  • 该服务可用于编辑和生成无限的文档，无需 AppSeed 帐户。 编辑器支持编辑全局 API 信息（标题、API 版本、描述）、添加/删除实体以及每个 OpenAPI 实体的字段。 支持的字段：

    • 用户可以直接编辑生成的 JSON，并且更改应自动反映在实体编辑器中。 一旦用户完成文档并点击下载按钮， OpenAPI定义就会保存在本地。

      谢谢阅读！ 如需更多OpenAPI相关资源，请随时访问：



原文:

Hello Coders!

This article presents OpenAPI Editor, a free, online tool that developers can use to edit their API interfaces. For newcomers, OpenAPI is an open-source format for designing RESTful APIs and web services that serves as a single source of truth for all parties involved in the software development process: developers, testers, etc.

Thanks for reading!

  • 👉 OpenAPI Editor - product page
  • 👉 Free support via Email and Discord

✨ Product Information

The service can be used to edit and generate unlimited documents, without an account on AppSeed. The editor supports editing the global API information (Title, API version, description), add/remove entities, and fields for each OpenAPI entity. Supported fields:

  • ✔️ string
  • ✔️ timestamp
  • ✔️ UUID
  • ✔️ binary
  • ✔️ boolean
  • ✔️ number
  • ✔️ object (all entities previously defined)

Users are able to edit directly the generated JSON and the changes should be reflected automatically in the entities editor. Once the user finishes the document and hits the download button, the OpenAPI definition is saved locally.

Thanks for reading! For more OpenAPI-related resources, feel free to access:

Items.Title: 我加入了 Forem 团队！ 🌱 🌱 | I've joined the Forem team! 🌱
Items.Description:
大家好！ 我很高兴分享我已全职加入Forem担任社交媒体经理🎉

在这个新职位上，我将主要负责监督 Forem、DEV 和 CodeNewbie 的社交媒体渠道的运营，以及跨平台开发和执行内容策略。

关于我的一点...

你是如何登陆 Forem 的？

在担任此职位之前，我曾担任自由数字策略师一年半，在我的自由职业生涯开始时，我兼职加入了 DEV 内容协调团队。 自从我开始工作以来，我真的很喜欢与团队合作，阅读你所有的帖子，并在社交上提升我的最爱——现在我可以做得更多！ 在我从事自由职业之前，我曾在一家电子学习机构担任内容作家（同时为我朋友的乐队做一些营销和公关工作😉）。 在此之前，我在我的家乡美国密歇根州卡拉马祖市的卡拉马祖学院学习新闻和媒体研究的同时，在商业广播电台和当地小企业的社交和内容方面实习。

你为什么选择社交媒体这个职业？

太有活力了！ 它需要大量的快速思考、创造力、战略以及从上述战略转向的意愿。 我喜欢每天的工作都是新的挑战。

我也很感激我有机会每天出现在这些平台上，并以人性和同情心领导。 我并不幻想今天的社交媒体环境都是小狗和彩虹 GIF！ 外面可能真的很艰难！ 我的目标始终是利用我必须减少的力量——尤其是对于那些被这些平台边缘化的用户。

如果您只能在 Twitter 上关注一 (1) 个人，那会是谁？

令人作呕的娱乐和教育编码变装皇后Anna Lytical ！ 她很凶猛，她很有趣，她就是当下。 作为互联网上的一个酷儿，她的内容总是让我感到被看到和被抓住。 好吧，关于我的事就够了！ 轮到你：

如果您只能在 Twitter 上关注一 (1) 个人，那会是谁？



原文:

Hey all! I'm delighted to share that I've joined Forem full-time in the role of Social Media Manager 🎉

In this new role, I'll primarily be overseeing operations for Forem, DEV, and CodeNewbie's social media channels, as well as developing and executing content strategies across platforms.

A little bit about me...

How did you land at Forem?

I worked as a freelance digital strategist for a year and a half prior to this role, and around the beginning of my freelance journey, I joined the DEV content coordination team part-time. I've really enjoyed working with the team, reading all your posts, and uplifting my favorites on social since I started — and now I get to do it even more! 🤩

Before my freelance career, I worked as a content writer for an eLearning agency (while doing some marketing and PR work for my friends' bands on the side 😉). Before that, I interned in social and content for commercial radio stations and local small businesses while studying journalism and media studies at Kalamazoo College in my home city of Kalamazoo, Michigan, USA.

Why did you choose a career in social media?

It's so dynamic! It requires a lot of quick thinking, creativity, strategy, and willingness to pivot from said strategy. I love that every day of work is a fresh challenge.

I also appreciate that I have the opportunity to show up on these platforms every day and lead with humanity and compassion. I am not under any illusion that today's social media landscape is all puppies and rainbow GIFs! It can be really tough out there! My goal is always to use the power I have to make it less so—especially for users who have been marginalized by these platforms.

If you could only follow one (1) person on Twitter, who would it be?

THE sickeningly entertaining & educational coding drag queen Anna Lytical! She's fierce, she's fun, she is the moment. As a fellow queer person on the internet, her content always makes me feel seen and held. 🏳️‍🌈💖

Ok, enough about me! Your turn:

If you could only follow one (1) person on Twitter, who would it be?

Items.Title: 我用过的最好的编码键盘 - 重温 | The best keyboard I ever had for coding - Revisited
Items.Description:
几个月前，他们发布了一个新版本，这次使用的是 GL 开关而不是 Romer G 开关，所以我再次购买了它 - 再次使用 clicky 开关 - 以测试差异。

用法几乎相同，但据称这款新 GL 更耐用等等。

两个键盘都和第一天一样好用，所以我想我得等着看 Romer G 能用多久。

自 2020 年 2 月以来对其进行测试后，我可以确保带有 Clicky 开关的罗技 G915 / 罗技 G915 TKL 是最好的编码键盘。

他们还交换了键帽上的材料，我不确定我是否喜欢。

自从我在 2022 年 1 月 25 日购买第二个 G915 TKL 以来，已经两年多了。

我在上一篇文章中没有提到的一件事是它有两种可能的联系。 通过加密狗和蓝牙。
这对我的日常使用特别有帮助，因为我使用蓝牙将它连接到公司的笔记本电脑上工作，然后我只需按一下按钮就可以切换到自己的电脑。

我可以确认低调的东西是必须的，我的手腕不再疼痛！

这次我也拿到了一张我现在的桌面的照片😁

你用什么键盘？ 您是否注意过工作场所的人体工程学和健康状况？

另外，如果您需要有关外围设备或硬件的帮助，请随时问我！



原文:

Previous post

Some months ago they released a new version, this time with GL switches instead Romer G ones, so I bought it -again with clicky switches- to test the differences.

The usage is almost the same but this new GL are allegedly more durable and so on.

Both keyboards work as fine as the first day so I guess I'll have to wait to see how much the Romer G ones lasts.

After testing it since February 2020 I can ensure that Logitech G915 / Logitech G915 TKL with Clicky switches is the best keyboard for coding.

They also swapped the material on the key caps which is something I'm not sure if I like or not.

It's been more than two years since I bought the first G915 TKL while I bought the second one in January 25th 2022.

One thing I didn't mention in the last post is that it has two possible connections. Through the dongle and through Bluetooth.
This is specially helpful in my daily usage as I connect it using Bluetooth to the company's laptop to work and then I can switch to my own computer just by hitting a button.

I can confirm that the low profile thingy is a must, no more pain in my wrists!

This time I also got a photo of my current desktop 😁

TL;DR
After more than 2 years I still think that this is the best keyboard I ever had for coding.


What keyboard do you use? Have you watched for ergonomics and health at your workspace?

Also if you need help with peripherals or hardware ask me anything!

Items.Title: JavaScript really needs a way to load local files. JavaScript 确实需要一种加载本地文件的方法。 . . . . | JavaScript really needs a way to load local files. . .
Items.Description:
卡林·贝南

发表于所以，我有点厌倦了 wiþ JavaScript。

我有一个（大型）应用程序的想法，但是，它需要我加载文件。 所以，我需要测试它是否可能，所以我写了一个快速测试： 

 fetch ( " test.txt " , { headers : { " Content-Type " : " text/plain " }, mode : " no-cors " }). then ( r => r . ok ? r . text () : `Status ${ String ( r . status )} : ${ r . statusText || r . type } ` ). then ( text => alert ( text ));

而且......不出所料，它没有用。
现在，它已经有一段时间没有真正起作用了。

但是，我觉得 ð 很烦人，因为它是一个TEXT文件，JUST TEXT，而 CORS 不希望我访问它。
当然，在过去的 ðere 是“安全问题”wiþ ðis，但是，真的很难验证 ðat 文件是否与 ðe HTML/JS 文件相同的目录分支？

一个问题是...

现在，在你们都对我尖叫一个可能非常明显的答案之前，我看到了一个问题。
但是，我不认为 ðat 应该是一些阻止 ðis 功能的东西。
为什么？ 因为不盲目信任网页是常识，无论是否本地。

我得到它。 不是每个人都有常识。

但是来吧，如果我们一直在做 ðis 参数，我们就必须保护所有 ðat 代码可以做的事情，并且绝对每一次都要保护婴儿。

萨尔玛·阿拉姆-奈勒 - Apr 27 4月27日



原文:

Calin Baenen

Posted on Apr 29

So, I'm getting a bit fed up wiþ JavaScript.

I had an idea for a (mega-)application, but, it requires me to load files. So, I needed to test if it was possible, so I wrote a quick test:

fetch("test.txt", { headers: {"Content-Type": "text/plain"}, mode: "no-cors"
}).then( r => r.ok ? r.text() : `Status ${String(r.status)}: ${r.statusText || r.type}`
).then(text => alert(text));

And... Not to my surprise, it didn't work.
It has never really worked for a good while, now.

But, I find ðis very irritating because it's a TEXT file, JUST TEXT, and CORS does not want me to access it.
Sure, in ðe past ðere were "security issues" wiþ ðis, but, is it really ÐAT hard to validate ðat a file is wiþin ðe same directory-branch as ðe HTML/JS file?

One issue wiþ ðis...

Now, before you all scream a possibly very obvious answer at me, I see an issue.
Ðe HTML file could just tell or coerce ðe person viewing it to paste some of ðeir documents in ðe folder ðe HTML file is in.

BUT, I don't þink ðat should be someþing preventing ðis feature.
Why? Because it's common sense to not blindly trust a webpage, local or not.

I get it. Not everyone has common sense.

But come on, if we made ðis argument all ðe time, we'd have to secure everyþing ðat code could do, and baby-proof absolutely everyþing.

Þank you for attending my rant on JavaScript.

Hemanshu Upadhyay - Apr 10

Salma Alam-Naylor - Apr 27

Items.Title: 关于锐化你的工具 | On Sharpening Your Tools
Items.Description:
Save some time to dream Save some time for yourself Don't let your time slip away Or be stolen by somebody else
忏悔的时候，小时候，我清楚地记得用我父亲的一把凿子作为螺丝刀。 我想我应该为此道歉。

昨天我收到了一封可爱的电子邮件，其中包含以下问题：

程序员花费一些工作时间来修复/锐化他们的工具是否很常见？

当我写下我的回复时，我请求允许引用他们的问题。 发件人有义务，所以这就是我写的（经过一些编辑以反映更广泛的受众）。

修复/锐化工具的价值

这是一个很好的问题，我有两种思路。

首先，我不知道您是否听说过 DEV，但这类问题是社区喜欢与之互动的问题。 我为Forem工作，为 DEV 提供支持的软件的维护者； 所以我对派人去那里有点偏见。

其次，让我们看看和我的具体情况和理解。

我假设在使用我的工具的过程中，我会探索这些工具以获得进一步的实用性或“生产力”收益。 这建立在我正在寻找结果而不是在座位/键盘上的想法的基础上。 请知道，我认为绝对令人愉快的一个结果是简单地玩电脑。 就像用刀无所事事地削木头一样令人愉快； 虽然有形的结果是一根尖棒，但无形的结果是一种惊奇感。 举个例子，我花了大约 2 个小时编写了一个 emacs lisp 函数，它帮助我整理了我们的拉取请求模板和提交消息的混合）。 花时间来做这件事有（或曾经？）一些结果：

首先，该解决方案为我每次拉取请求节省了大约 20 秒的时间。 自编写该功能以来，我已使用该功能大约 150 次，因此花费 2 小时节省了 50 分钟。 此外，节省下来的 20 秒还包括微决策。

通过自动化其中一些任务，我减少了一个可能导致决策疲劳的位置。

其次，我现在有一个关于如何做到这一点或什么是可能的心理途径； 这帮助我快速编写了一个脚本，以方便编写周末报告（10 分钟左右？）。

我现在每周使用这个新功能大约 3 次。 我可能会节省 30 秒的复制粘贴时间以及多个工具的上下文切换。

这种模式在其他任务中重复出现。

我早就映射了Cmd + . 在规范/测试和文件之间切换。 仅此一项就帮助我始终思考我的测试以及维持该路径所需的可预测文件系统结构。

我看过其他开发人员打开他们的项目导航树，然后单击文件夹以进入相关测试。 这会消耗决策资源。

我最近在安装tmr.el ，这是一个 Emacs 的计时器包。 为什么？ 因为我现在可以轻松地在 Emacs 中设置计时器。 我不需要拿起我的手机（这种不同的行为已经打破了我的思维）。

我预计会经常使用tmr.el吗？ 不，因为我在电脑前不经常需要计时器。 但它就在那里。 我练习设置它。

使用 Emacs 的最后一个案例是我为帮助我的 org-roam contexts所做的工作。 我可以选择一组标签（或一组命名的标签）来自动过滤我的 Org-Roam 命令：捕获、查找、插入或CaPF 。

这意味着我可以轻松地在同一目录中编写工作笔记和播放笔记。 然后，我既可以互连这些笔记，又可以帮助我不要“意外地”互连它们。 这有助于我删除决策点。

它还帮助我开始将我的博客文章从独立的 Hugo 文件转移到我的PKM系统中。

当我在处理一个我很好理解的问题时，我会尝试对我的编辑器进行所有这些“探索”。

当我试图通过更复杂或更模糊的事情进行推理时，我会避免尝试同时深入我的文本编辑器。 但我可能会快速记下告诉自己“嘿，你做了这件事……也许在你完成后花点时间考虑一下。”

我希望这能提供一些背景信息，说明您个人为什么要这样做，以及如何不是请求许可，而是让许可成为您的软件开发过程的一部分。

以一个问题结尾

现在我想知道，你在磨砺你的工具方面学到了什么？

学分

原文: 
Save some time to dream
Save some time for yourself
Don't let your time slip away
Or be stolen by somebody else
— John Mellencamp, Save Some Time to Dream

Confession time, as a child, I have a distinct memory of using one of my father’s chisels as a screw driver. I suppose I should apologize for that.

Yesterday I received a lovely email that included the following question:

Is it common for programmers to spend a bit of their working hours fixing/sharpening their tools?

As I wrote up my response, I asked for permission to reference their question. The sender obliged, so here’s what I wrote (with some editing to reflect a broader audience).

The Value of Fixing/Sharpening Your Tools

This is a great question and I have two trains of thought.

First, I don’t know if you’ve heard of DEV but this kind of question is one that the community loves to interact with. I work for the Forem, the maintainers of the software that powers DEV; so I’m a bit biased on sending people there.

Second, let’s look and my specific situation and understanding.

It is my assumption that in the process of using my tools, I explore those tools for further utility or “productivity” gains. This builds on the idea that I am looking for outcomes not time in seat/at keyboard. And please know that one outcome I consider absolutely enjoyable is the puttering of time simply playing with a computer. Much like idly whittling wood with a knife is quite enjoyable; and while the tangible outcome is a pointy stick, the intangible is a sense of wonder. A case in point, I spent about 2 hours writing an emacs lisp function that helped me tidy up the the blend of our pull request template and the commit message). There are (or were?) a few outcomes of taking time to do that:

First, the solution saves me about 20 seconds of time each pull request. I’ve used that function about 150 times since writing it, so I’m at 50 minutes saved with a 2 hour spend. In addition, those 20 seconds saved also included micro-decisions.

By automating a bit of those tasks, I’ve reduced one location that might cause decision fatigue.

Second, I now have a mental pathway of how to do this or what might be possible; which helped me quickly write a script to facilitate writing end of week reports (10 minutes or so?).

I now use that new function about 3 times a week. I likely saves me 30 seconds of copy paste plus the context switching of multiple tools.

This pattern repeats itself in other tasks.

I have long mapped Cmd + . to toggle between spec/test and file. That alone has helped me always think about my test as well as the predictable file system structure necessary to sustain that pathway.

I have watched other developers open their project navigation tree, and click through folders to get to the related test. That consumes decision-making resources.

I recently fiddled with installing tmr.el, a timer package for Emacs. Why? Because I can now easily set a timer within Emacs. I don’t need to grab my phone (which the vary act of doing already breaks my mental thinking).

Do I anticipate using tmr.el much? No, because it’s not often that I want a timer while I’m at my computer. But it’s there. And I practiced setting it.

A final case of working with Emacs is work I did to help with my org-roam contexts. I can select a set of tags (or a named group of tags) to auto-filter my Org-Roam commands: capture, find, insert, or CaPF.

What this has meant is I can easily write work notes and play notes in the same directory. I can then both interconnect those notes but also help me not “accidentally” inter-connect them. This helps me remove a decision-making point.

It has also helped me begin moving my blog posts from stand-alone Hugo files into my PKM system.

And I try to do all of this “exploration” of my editor when I’m working on a well understood to me problem.

When I’m trying to reason through something more complicated or vague, I avoid trying to also dive into my text editor. But I might make a quick note to tell myself “Hey you did this thing…maybe spend some time thinking about it after you’re done.”

I hope this provides some context on why you personally would want to do it, and how it’s not about asking for permission but to instead have that be part of your software development process.

Ending with a Question

Now I’m wondering, what have you learned in sharpening your tools?

Items.Title: 帮助我成为 Google Cloud 客户，赚取 10 美元。 | Help me become a Google Cloud customer, earn $10.
Items.Description:
这不是一个玩笑。 我已经尝试成为 Google Cloud 客户 9 周，但没有成功。 谷歌支持帮不上忙。 我很绝望，所以我承诺通过 PayPal 向你支付 10 美元，如果你能帮助我在https://console.cloud.google.com/freetrial上开始我的 Google Cloud 试用

你可能会问。 好吧，这里有个问题：付款必须与我刚刚成立 4 个月的新创业公司相关联。 我的银行说他们不向成立不到一年的初创公司提供信用卡。 所以我的付款方式是：

  • 德国银行账户（SEPA 直接借记）
  • 贝宝账户

错误代码

  • 以字面意思是“信用卡或借记卡”的形式

谷歌支持

多么令人失望。 由于某些莫名其妙的原因，大多数支持人员并不关心错误代码。 即使在 2 周没有任何进展并直接询问错误代码的含义之后，他们也只是无视了我的请求。 谷歌支持是否可能不知道他们的错误代码是什么意思......？ 我与以下支持部门合作：

  • 谷歌云
  • 谷歌支付
  • 他们所有人都只是对屏幕截图感兴趣，并给出了诸如“尝试不同的浏览器”和“清除缓存”之类的随机建议。 我两个都做了，但没什么区别。 另一个令人失望的是：没有支持团队可以访问其他团队的案例，所以我不得不从一开始就多次解释一切。 联系了 GCP 的升级经理，但即使是那个人也告诉我他无法提供任何帮助。

    免责声明

    这不是咆哮。 我仍然相信谷歌拥有优秀的软件工程师和优秀的产品。 特别是他们的云产品基于深思熟虑的架构，能够在全球范围内有效地扩展。 尽管我对 Google 的钦佩性受到了很大影响，但我仍然更喜欢使用 GCP 而不是 AWS、Azure 或任何其他云。



原文:

This is not a joke. I've been trying to become a Google Cloud customer for 9 weeks, but without success. Google support couldn't help. I'm desperate, so hereby I promise to pay you $10 via PayPal if I you can help me to start my Google Cloud trial on https://console.cloud.google.com/freetrial

"What can be so hard?" you might ask. Well, here's the catch: payments must be linked to my new startup, which is just 4 months old. My bank says they don't provide credit cards to startups that exist for less than a year. So my payments options are:

  • bank account in Germany (SEPA direct debit)
  • VISA debit card
  • PayPal account

The Error Codes

  • OR-BSBBF-88 (when I try to verify a bank account in GCP)
  • OR-RWE-02 (when I try to verify a bank account in Google Pay)
  • OR-ACH-04 (for PayPal after returning from successful PayPal login)
  • "Prepaid cards cannot be used." in the form that literally says "credit or debit cards"

All errors are 100% reproducible with Chrome and Firefox.

Google Support

What a disappointment. For some inexplicable reason most support people didn't care about error codes. Even after 2 weeks without any progress and directly asking for the meaning of error codes they just ignored my plea. Is it possible that Google support has no clue what their error codes mean...? I worked with the following support departments:

  • Google Cloud
  • Google Pay
  • Google Play (I think my case was routed there by mistake)

All of them were just interested in screenshots and gave random suggestions like "try a different browser" and "clear cache". I did both, but it made no difference. Another disappointment: no support team has access to the other team's cases, so I had to explain everything from the start multiple times. An escalation manager of GCP was contacted, but even that guy told me he cannot help in any way.

Disclaimer

This is not rant. I still believe that Google has great software engineers and good products. Especially their cloud offerings are based on a well thought out architecture, which is able to efficiently scale globally. Even though my admiration for Google has suffered a lot, I'd still prefer to use GCP instead of AWS, Azure, or any other cloud.
Items.Author: thorstenhirsch

Items.Title: useContext 的案例 | The Case for useContext
Items.Description:
如果您在 9 个月零 13 天前和我一样，那么您可能正在以工程师 I 的身份加入您的第一个项目，低调紧张，因为您将接受语言和/或非常困难的任务你不知道你会使用的图书馆。 坐下，系好安全带，呼吸； 这很正常！ 我在这里发布好消息：如果你偷偷摸摸的那个库是 React，我会在这里提供帮助。

在我们特别深入useContext之前，让我们先简短地（我的意思是breif ）沿着内存通道漫步。 这意味着您可以在组件安装时获取某些内容，或者在组件卸载时销毁订阅。 伟大的概念； 凌乱的实施。 然而，他们最终在实现大转折的过程中发布了基于function的组件。 其中，也是我们今天的主题，是useContext 。

它是什么？

这是一个钩子，可让您使用Context.Provider中的值。 该值可以保存一个全局状态并将其提供给任何想要接入的组件。所需要的只是组件由提供者包装，并调用useContext(context) 。 不过，React 自己的文档将首先告诉您，在使用它之前要考虑其他选项。 他们并没有真正深入研究它，但有人推测这可能是重新渲染孩子的负担有多大，或者像在你的应用程序上嵌套大量上下文提供者一样简单变得荒谬。 无论如何，它都会引起初学者的一系列问题，例如“我什么时候使用上下文？”

但更细致入微的解释涉及将组件树可视化，类似于绘制家谱的方式。 您的应用程序位于顶部，每个分支向下拆分 n 次，直到您的所有子元素都被渲染。 现在，在该树结构中，隔离需要此状态的多个组件。 它们相隔多少级或“度”？ 从一个到另一个的路径是简单的，例如直接击落，还是复杂的，涉及通过多个级别的回调等？ 从这里开始，让你的直觉指导决定。 该路径是您的道具管道需求的直接可视化。 如果它看起来像一场噩梦，那就是。

导致我使用上下文的典型案例是我所说的“培育组件”。 这些组件没有可以为它们有效管理状态的共享直接或足够接近的父级。 我想停下来解释一下我是如何理解“组件化”UI 的。 我将 React 的组件分为三类：核心，例如按钮或输入； 复合，例如实现标题、按钮和图像组件的卡片； 和页面，或组合组件的编译和排列以创建 UI。

设置你的上下文

导航栏中的头像和用户设置页面都需要访问您当前的用户数据，但是由于您如何安排我的组件，您不知道吗，从一个到另一个的路径有点一场滚烫的噩梦。 让我们评估一下需求。 头像需要显示您的姓名和图像，设置页面需要所有当前设置，包括您当前的用户名和头像图像。 是的，您可以选择在每个组件中针对此信息进行单独的 API 调用，但是当您在设置中更新它时，在您刷新单页应用程序之前，它不会在您的应用栏中发生变化，除非您以某种方式构建设置页面调用来更新头像的奇怪刷新触发器......是的，它只是一团糟。 更好的主意：让我们创建一个用户数据上下文，它不仅可以处理传递该信息，还可以处理 API 接口！

 /* Start by defining your interfaces and types.*/ type OnOff = " on " | " off " interface UserSettings { notifications : OnOff , emails : OnOff } interface User { username : string , avatarUri : string , settings : UserSettings } interface IUserContext extends User { update : ( user : User ) => HttpResponse } /* This variable now holds our context, including the * initial state which we pass in as the IUserContext * object. * * All of this is placeholder, typically. */ const UserContext = createContext < IUserContext > ({ username : " testUser " , avatarUri : " https://cdn.xyz.test/testUser/12345 " , settings : { notifications : " off " , emails : " off " }, update : ( v : User ) => 201 // Just a placeholder })

为孩子提供价值观

下一步是设置提供程序。 这是一个组件，它将把你的树包装在任何必要的层，以实现上下文状态在所有需要它的组件之间的分布。 由于这些情况通常是针对不相关或深度嵌套的组件，因此大多数情况下，如果不包装整个应用程序，那么预计这将处于相当高的水平。

您的提供者将在本教程中处理两件事：API 调用和应用程序对状态数据的更改。 我们希望在用户调用update函数时更新来自 API 的数据。 我们希望有效负载具有反应性，并使用新数据进行更新。 这意味着如果我们要更新我们假设的头像Uri，在我们点击保存并且我们的调用完成后，我们假设的导航栏中的头像应该改变！

 const UserProvider = ( props : PropsWithChildren < {} > ) => { /* This is our API call. If you aren't using hook-based * API solutions yet, get on it! */ const [ data , updateApi ] = useApi < User > ( " /users/testUser " ) /* Because the state is complex, we should use a reducer * rather than useState. This is fine for now. * * I can do a whole other article on the * case for useReducer another time! */ const [ payload , updatePayload ] = useState < Partial < IUserContext >> ({ username : "" , avatarUri : "" , settings : { notifications : " off " , emails : " off " }, /* When the user calls this, they'll send the current * payload values through an UPDATE call and make the * back-end changes. That'll refresh the data variable */ update : updateApi }) /* Whenever the data variable refreshes, the payload will be * updated, and reactively, children nested and consuming this * value will also update. */ useEffect (() => { updatePayload ({ username : data . username , avatarUri : data . avatarUri , settings : data . settings }) }, [ data ]) /* This is the JSX that'll wrap all children and provide the value * to them through the useContext hook. */ return ( < UserContext . Provider value = { payload } > { props . children } < /UserContext.Provider > ) }

其余的很容易，真的。 只需在由该上下文包装的组件中通过useContext(UserContext)使用该上下文，并随心所欲地使用该状态。

 const Avatar = () => { /* Hook into the context */ const userContext = useContext ( UserContext ) /* Pass the state uri value in an API call */ const [ data ] = useApi ( userContext . avatarUri ) /* Render it out! */ return ( < span > { data } < /span > ) }

作者注

感谢您在忙碌的一天中抽出时间阅读本文。 这是我想尝试的新事物，因为我发现自己在工作中一直在构建有趣的小解决方案，作为很好的教学材料。 如果您想了解更多这些内容，例如关于 React 的基于钩子的 API 接口，或者更深入地了解组件化接口的思维方式，请告诉我！



原文:

If you're anything like me 9 months and 13 days ago, you're probably onboarding to your first project as an Engineer I, low-key nervous that you're going to get tasked with something incredibly difficult in a language and/or library you didn't know you'd be using. Sit down, buckle up, and breathe; this is normal! I'm here baring good news: if that library that snuck up on you is React, I'm here to help.

Before we dig into useContext in particular, let's take a brief (and I mean breif) stroll down memory lane. React started with a Class-based component system that provided methods to perform operations during the lifecycle of the component. This meant you can fetch something when a component mounted, or destroy subscriptions when it unmounted. Great concept; messy implementation. However, they eventually released function-based components along the way to the big turning point. React 16.8 drops, and suddenly, you've got hooks. Hooks provide you access to tinker with the tools, dare I say the superpowers, of the React library. Among these, and our topic for today, is useContext.

What is it?

It's a hook that lets you consume a value from a Context.Provider. This value can hold a global state and provide it to any components that want to tap in. All that's required is for the component to be wrapped by the provider, and for it to call useContext(context). React's own docs will be the first to tell you, though, to consider other options before using this. They don't really dig into it, but some speculate it could be how taxing the re-render of children is, or as simple as nesting a ton of context providers over your app gets ridiculous. Whatever the case, it's led to a slew of questions from beginners like, "when do I use a context?"

TL;DR - Whenever piping or passing props becomes incredibly tiresome to maintain.

But a more nuanced explanation involves visualizing your component tree similar to how a family tree is drawn. Your app is the top, and each branch down splits n-many times until all your children elements are rendered. Now, in that tree structure, isolate the multiple components that need this state. How many levels or "degrees" apart are they? Is the path from one to the other simple, such as a straight shot down, or complex, involving callbacks through multiple levels, etc? From here, let your intuition guide the decision. That path is a direct visualization of your prop piping needs. If it looks like a nightmare, it will be.

The typical case that leads me to use a context is what I refer to as "foster components". These components have no shared direct or close-enough parent that can manage the state efficiently for them. For a brief moment, I'd like to stop and explain how I make sense of a "componentized" UI. I've broken React's components up into three categories: core, such as buttons or inputs; composite, such as a card that implements title, button, and image components; and pages, or compilations and arrangements of composite components to create a UI.

Setup your context

An avatar in your navigation bar, and the user settings page, both would need access to your current user data, but due to how you arranged my components, wouldn't y'know it, the path from one to the other is a bit of a piping nightmare. Let's assess the needs. The avatar would need your name and image to display, and the settings page would need all current settings, including your current username and avatar image. Yes, you can opt to make separate API calls in each component for this information, but as you update it in your settings, it wouldn't change in your app bar until you refreshed the single-page application, unless you built in some kinda weird refresh trigger that the settings page called to update the avatar in the... yeah, it's just a mess. Better idea: let's make a user data context that not only handles passing that info around, but can handle the API interfacing as well!

/* Start by defining your interfaces and types.*/
type OnOff = "on" | "off"
interface UserSettings { notifications: OnOff, emails: OnOff
} interface User { username: string, avatarUri: string, settings: UserSettings
} interface IUserContext extends User { update: (user: User) => HttpResponse
} /* This variable now holds our context, including the
* initial state which we pass in as the IUserContext
* object. * * All of this is placeholder, typically. */
const UserContext = createContext<IUserContext>({ username: "testUser", avatarUri: "https://cdn.xyz.test/testUser/12345", settings: { notifications: "off", emails: "off" }, update: (v: User) => 201 // Just a placeholder
})

Provide the values to children

The next step is setting up a provider. This is the component that'll wrap your tree at whatever layer necessary to achieve distribution of the context state across all components that need it. Since these cases are often for unrelated or deeply nested components, expect this to be at a pretty high level, if not wrapping the whole app, most of the time.

Your provider is going to handle two things in this tutorial: the API call and the app's changes to the data in state. We want the data from the API to update when the user calls an update function. We want the payload to be reactive and also update with the new data. This means that if we are to update our hypothetical avatarUri, after we hit save and our call completes, the avatar in our hypothetical navigation bar should change!

const UserProvider = (props: PropsWithChildren<{}>) => { /* This is our API call. If you aren't using hook-based * API solutions yet, get on it! */ const [data, updateApi] = useApi<User>("/users/testUser") /* Because the state is complex, we should use a reducer * rather than useState. This is fine for now. * * I can do a whole other article on the * case for useReducer another time! */ const [payload, updatePayload] = useState<Partial<IUserContext>>({ username: "", avatarUri: "", settings: { notifications: "off", emails: "off" }, /* When the user calls this, they'll send the current * payload values through an UPDATE call and make the * back-end changes. That'll refresh the data variable */ update: updateApi }) /* Whenever the data variable refreshes, the payload will be * updated, and reactively, children nested and consuming this * value will also update. */ useEffect(() => { updatePayload({ username: data.username, avatarUri: data.avatarUri, settings: data.settings }) }, [data]) /* This is the JSX that'll wrap all children and provide the value * to them through the useContext hook. */ return ( <UserContext.Provider value={payload}> {props.children} </UserContext.Provider>
 )
}

The rest is easy, really. Simply consume the context via useContext(UserContext) in components wrapped by this context, and use the state however you please.

const Avatar = () => { /* Hook into the context */ const userContext = useContext(UserContext) /* Pass the state uri value in an API call */ const [data] = useApi(userContext.avatarUri) /* Render it out! */ return ( <span>{data}</span>
 )
}

Author's note

Thanks for taking the time out of your busy day to read this. It's something new I wanted to try since I find myself building fun little solutions all the time at work that serve as great teaching material. If you'd like to see more of these, such as one about hook-based API interfaces for React, or a deeper breakdown of the mentality about componentized interfaces, let me know!

Items.Title: VMware NSX-T 和 Cisco 建模实验室的网络实验 | Network Experiments with VMware NSX-T and Cisco Modeling Labs
Items.Description:
事实证明，Cisco Modeling Labs (CML) 是部署虚拟网络资源的绝佳工具，但“只有 Cisco VNF”的限制有点多。

让我们借此机会真正利用 NSX-T 为虚拟网络实验室提供的功能！

概述

出于实验室建设的目的，我将使用现有的 Tier-0 路由器和上行链路来促进“基础”，例如互联网连接和远程访问：

构建 NSX-T“外部”环境

我们特别需要一个来执行这个 - Q-in-VNI封装- 它是在每个段的基础上启用的。 与 NSX-V 相比，这简单明了 - 用户只需将允许的 VLAN 范围应用于 vn-segment，在这种情况下，_ * eng-lab-vn-cml-trunk: * _

我们需要通过创建和应用以下分段配置文件来禁用一些保护网络的安全功能。 目的是限制 ARP 侦听并强制 NSX 通过网段而不是控制器学习 MAC 地址。


这一点我怎么强调都不为过，Q-in-VNI 功能为我们提供了与其他 VF 几乎无限的功率，绕过 vSphere 中 10 个 vNIC 的限制，并减少了必须部署到通常是“物理管道”的段的数量.

这是通过 API 的样子：

公开与 CML 的连接

在 NSX 中生成段已完成，现在我们需要使用 CML 深入了解一下。 让我们将这些段添加到适当的 VM：

我们将创建两个段作为桥接适配器：

一句警告——这是一个Linux 软件桥，它将复制流经端口的所有内容。 在实验室中利用 CML 连接

最困难的部分完成了！ 要使用这个新网络，请添加一个External Connector类型的节点，并将其配置为利用我们刚刚创建的网桥：

下一步将是部署资源并将它们配置为连接到这个中心点——我建议使用这种方法使节点可以通过它们的管理接口访问，即部署一个管理 VRF 并将所有节点连接到它以进行诸如 Ansible 测试之类的事情。 得到教训

这是一个网络实验室，在构建这种自由形式的东西时，我们正在拆除一些护栏。 从主机向外发送垃圾邮件 BPDU 或数据包泛滥的固有危险可以通过内置功能“分段安全配置文件”来控制，这表明 VMware 明确打算在未来支持类似的用例。

如果在第 1 层的路由模式下，NSX 还会启用一些其他功能。 设置 NAT 策略，将路由重新分配为第 1 层静态，以控制您可能希望或可能不希望接触“真实网络”的任何内部连接的导出，这些都是微不足道的。

我确实认为这种组合对于支持企业部署来说是一个非常令人印象深刻的一二打一拳。 如果你问思科以外的任何人，典型的企业网络环境将涉及许多不同的网络供应商，并且在“测试环境”中具有混合和匹配的灵活性是大多数基础设施工程师没有但应该拥有的奢侈品。

这是我为测试此功能而部署的 CML 场景：



原文:

Cisco Modeling Labs (CML) has turned out to be a great tool for deploying virtual network resources, but the "only Cisco VNFs" limitation is a bit much.

Let's use this opportunity to really take advantage of the capabilities that NSX-T has for virtual network labs!

Overview

For the purpose of lab construction, I will use an existing Tier-0 router and uplinks to facilitate the "basics", e.g. internet connectivity && remote accessibility:

Constructing the NSX-T "Outside" Environment

NSX-T has a few super-powers when it comes to constructing network topologies. We need one in particular to execute this - Q-in-VNI encapsulation - which is enabled on a per-segment basis. Compared to NSX-V, this is simple and straightforward - a user simply applies the allowed VLAN range to the vn-segment, in this case, _ *eng-lab-vn-cml-trunk: * _

We'll need to disable some security features that protect the network by creating and applying the following segment profiles. The objective is to limit ARP snooping and force NSX to learn MAC addresses over the segment instead of from the controller.


I can't stress this enough, the Q-in-VNI feature gives us near unlimited power with other VFs, circumventing the 10 vNIC limit in vSphere and reducing the number of segments that must be deployed to what would normally be "physical pipes".

Here's what it looks like via the API:

Expose connectivity to CML

Generating the segments in NSX is complete, and now we need to get into the weeds a bit with CML. CML is, in essence, a Linux machine running containers, and has external interface support. Let's add the segments to the appropriate VM:

CML provides a Cockpit UI to make this easy, but the usual suspects exist to create a Linux bridge. We're going to create both segments as bridge adapters:

A word of warning - this is a Linux Software Bridge , and will copy everything flowing through the port. NSX helps by squelching a significant chunk of the "network storms" that result, but I would recommend not putting it on the same NSX manager or hosts as your production environments.

Leveraging CML connectivity in a Lab

The hard part's done! To consume this new network, add a node of type External Connector , and configure it to leverage the bridge we just created:

The next step would be to deploy resources and configure them to connect to this central point - I'd recommend this approach to make nodes reachable via their management interface, i.e. deploy a management VRF and connect all nodes to it for stuff like Ansible testing. CML has an API, so the end goal here would be to spin up a test topology and test code against it, or to build a mini service provider.

Lessons Learned

This is a network lab, and we're taking some of the guardrails off when building something this free-form. NSX does allow an engineer to do so on a per-segment basis , and insulates the "outside" network from any weird stuff you intend to do. The inherent dangers to spamming BPDUs outwards from a host or packet flooding can be contained with the built-in feature "segment security profiles", indicating that VMware had clear intent to support similar use-cases in the future.

NSX also enables a few other functions if in routed mode with a Tier-1. It's trivial to set up NAT policies, redistribute routes as Tier-1 statics, to control export of any internal connectivity you may or may not want touching the "real network".

I do think that this combination is a really impressive one-two punch for supporting enterprise deployments. If you ask anyone but Cisco, a typical enterprise network environment will involve many different network vendors, and having the flexibility to mix and match in a "testing environment" is a luxury that most infrastructure engineers don't have, but should.

原文:

Continuando o ultimo post sobre os Problems Set a respeito da Primeira semana, vamos começar a resolução do Cash (Greedy Algorithms).

Cash: Simples

Este problema implica em ajudar os vendedores dar o troco para os compradores de forma facilitada. Tem em seu projeto ajudar os caixas a dar o menor numero de moedas como troco.

Pseudocodigo

  • Perguntar ao usuário o valor do troco
  • Se o valor for inferior a 0, perguntar de novo
  • Utilizar o tipo de dados INT
  • Manter registro das moedas usadas
  • Exibir o número total de moedas

Resolução 

#include <cs50.h>
#include <stdio.h>

int get_cents(void);
int calculate_quarters(int cents);
int calculate_dimes(int cents);
int calculate_nickels(int cents);
int calculate_pennies(int cents); int main(void)
{ // Ask how many cents the customer is owed int cents = get_cents(); // Calculate the number of quarters to give the customer int quarters = calculate_quarters(cents); cents = cents - quarters * 25; // Calculate the number of dimes to give the customer int dimes = calculate_dimes(cents); cents = cents - dimes * 10; // Calculate the number of nickels to give the customer int nickels = calculate_nickels(cents); cents = cents - nickels * 5; // Calculate the number of pennies to give the customer int pennies = calculate_pennies(cents); cents = cents - pennies * 1; // Sum coins int coins = quarters + dimes + nickels + pennies; // Print total number of coins to give the customer printf("%i\n", coins);
} int get_cents(void)
{ int cents = 0; do { cents = get_int("Change owed: "); } while (cents < 0); return cents;
} int calculate_quarters(int cents)
{ return cents / 25;
} int calculate_dimes(int cents)
{ return cents / 10;
} int calculate_nickels(int cents)
{ return cents / 5;
} int calculate_pennies(int cents)
{ return cents;
}

A main já vem completa, cabendo a você apenas completar a lógica das funções que efetuaram a lógica.

Explicação

No primeiro momento, o usuário informa o valor do troco.

A função int get_cents(void) armazena o valor dentro da variável cents e retorna esse valor com um tipo de dado INT, que corresponde aos números inteiros.

Em seguida, procede para a instrução debaixo, int calculate_quarters(int cents).

Ao fazer a divisão 41 / 25, o valor em uma calculadora dará 1.64. Mas dado a natureza dessa função retornar um INT, retornará apenas 1. O motivo disso é porque INT corresponde aos valores inteiros apenas, não armazenando as cadas decimais, para isto nós temos um outro tipo de dado, o FLOAT.

E assim,

// Calculate the number of quarters to give the customer int quarters = calculate_quarters(cents); cents = cents - quarters * 25;

quarters = 1;
cents = 41 - (1 * 25)
= 16

E assim se segue para a próxima instrução seguindo a mesma lógica acima, até encontrar o valor final de 4 moedas.

Conclusão

Esse conjunto de problemas é importante para conhecer e aprender sobre funções e as particularidades da linguagem C, a importância de que tipo de dado será usado para criar funções e como isso afetará o retorno delas.

Items.Description:
托马斯·韦格扎诺夫斯基

发表于我尝试了各种工具从heavymetal DLC 中提取数据。

不团结

首先我尝试了 DisUnity - 但这是一个废弃的项目，它不适用于当前版本。

接下来我尝试了Unity Asset Bundle Extractor ，它非常不直观，但它提取了我想要的东西。 然后我按下“导出原始”。

不幸的是，它创建了像这样的文件： weapon/autocannon_lb10x_0_stock.json weapon_autocannon_lb10x_0_stock.json-CAB_81519cd4dc355e215c567f814d1dd677--9084477644472635856.dat 。

他们在每个文件的开头和结尾也有一些奇怪的废话。 剥离不会太难，但我希望得到正确的数据。

最后“插件>导出到.txt”工作。

组织文件

这些文件仍然具有愚蠢的名称，例如weapon_autocannon_lb10x_0_stock.json-CAB_81519cd4dc355e215c567f814d1dd677--9084477644472635856.txt 。

所以我做了一个快速的：

 $ rename 's/-CAB_.*//' *.txt

之后我将武器文件移动到了heavymetal/weapon/*.json 。 稍后我可能需要组织其他文件，例如弹药等。

幸运的是，这是唯一带有额外武器的 DLC。 如果我想分析所有机甲，我需要对其他 DLC 重复相同的步骤。

脚本

#!/usr/bin/env ruby require "json" require "memoist" require "pathname" require "pry" class Weapon extend Memoist attr_reader :data def initialize ( path ) @data = JSON . parse ( path . read ) end memoize def name bonuses = [ bonus_a , bonus_b ]. compact if bonuses . empty? base_name else " #{ base_name } ( #{ bonuses . join ( ", " ) } )" end end memoize def base_name [ data [ "Description" ][ "Name" ], data [ "Description" ][ "UIName" ], ]. compact . last . gsub ( " +" , "+" ) end memoize def bonus_a data [ "BonusValueA" ] == "" ? nil : data [ "BonusValueA" ]. gsub ( /[az]\K\./ , "" ) end memoize def bonus_b data [ "BonusValueB" ] == "" ? nil : data [ "BonusValueB" ]. gsub ( /[az]\K\./ , "" ) end def category @data [ "Category" ] end def tonnage @data [ "Tonnage" ] end def damage shots * base_damage end def base_damage @data [ "Damage" ] end def shots @data [ "ShotsWhenFired" ] end def heat @data [ "HeatGenerated" ] end def heat_tonnage heat / 3.0 end def ammo_tonnage # TODO 0 end def total_tonnage tonnage + heat_tonnage + ammo_tonnage end def ammo_category @data [ "ammoCategoryID" ] end def purchasable? @data [ "Description" ][ "Purchasable" ] end end class BattleTechGame extend Memoist def initialize ( game_root , * dlc_roots ) @game_root = Pathname ( game_root ) @dlc_roots = dlc_roots . map { | path | Pathname ( path )} end memoize def data_root @game_root + "BattleTech_Data/StreamingAssets/data" end memoize def weapon_files [ data_root , * @dlc_roots ] . flat_map { | root | root . glob ( "weapon/*.json" )} . select { | n | n . basename . to_s != "WeaponTemplate.json" } end memoize def weapons weapon_files . map { | path | Weapon . new ( path )} end def print_weapons_report weapons . select { | w | w . category != "Melee" and w . name != "AI Laser" } . sort_by { | w | [ w . damage . to_f / w . total_tonnage , w . damage , w . name ] } . reverse . each { | w | puts "* #{ w . name } : #{ w . damage } / #{ w . total_tonnage . round ( 2 ) } " } end end game = BattleTechGame . new ( * ARGV ) game . print_weapons_report

要运行脚本，我使用./weapons /path/to/battletech /path/to/heavymetal

结果

这是结果。 由于脚本仍然没有计算弹药，它高估了使用武器的弹药。 任何具有特殊效果的武器（例如列表末尾的所有那些 TAG、NARC 和 COIL）也让它非常困惑。 最好将他们排除在分析之外。

  • 火焰喷射器+（* 4 弹药，+ 5 伤害）：10 / 1.0
  • 冷落 PPC++ (+ 10 Dmg): 125 / 17.67
  • 冷落 PPC+（+ 5 伤害）：100 / 17.67
  • 冷落 PPC++（- 10 热度）：75 / 14.33
  • 高斯步枪++（- 2 吨，- 1 个插槽）：75 / 14.67
  • 高斯步枪+（- 2 吨）：75 / 14.67
  • 火焰喷射器+++（* 40 弹药）：5 / 1.0
  • 火焰喷射器（* 4 弹药）：5 / 1.0
  • 冷落 PPC+（- 5 热度）：75 / 16.0
  • 高斯步枪：75 / 16.67
  • 冷落 PPC++ (+ 10 StbDmg): 75 / 17.67
  • 冷落 PPC+ (+ 5 StbDmg): 75 / 17.67
  • 冷落 PPC：75 / 17.67
  • 每次点击费用：50 / 18.67
  • 地狱火（* 8 发球）：4 / 4.0
  • 纳克信标（+ 25% 伤害标记，* 导弹）：1 / 3.0

到目前为止的故事

所有代码都在 GitHub 上。

接下来

在接下来的几集中，我们将通过在计数中加入弹药来完成脚本。



原文:

Tomasz Wegrzanowski

Posted on Apr 30

I tried various tools to extract data from heavymetal DLC.

DisUnity

First I tried DisUnity - but that's an abandoned project, and it doesn't work with current version.

Unity Asset Bundle Extractor

Next I tried Unity Asset Bundle Extractor which is very nonintuitive but it extracted what I want. "Select All" doesn't work, I needed to select first item, then shift click on the last item. Then I pressed "Export Raw".

It unfortunately created files like: weapon_autocannon_lb10x_0_stock.json-CAB_81519cd4dc355e215c567f814d1dd677--9084477644472635856.dat instead of weapon/autocannon_lb10x_0_stock.json.

They also some weird crap at start and end of each file. It wouldn't be too difficult to strip, but I hoped for just the proper data.

"Dump as text file" and "Dump as json file" options were even worse. Finally "Plugins > Export to .txt" worked.

Unity Asset Bundle Extractor has all information necessary to get it right, it just choses not to.

Organizing the files

The files still had stupid names like weapon_autocannon_lb10x_0_stock.json-CAB_81519cd4dc355e215c567f814d1dd677--9084477644472635856.txt.

So I did a quick:

$ rename 's/-CAB_.*//' *.txt

After that I moved weapon files to heavymetal/weapon/*.json. I might need to organize other files like ammo etc. later.

Fortunately that's the only DLC with extra weapons. If I wanted to analyze all mechs, I'd need to repeat the same steps with other DLCs.

Script 

#!/usr/bin/env ruby require "json"
require "memoist"
require "pathname"
require "pry" class Weapon extend Memoist attr_reader :data def initialize(path) @data = JSON.parse(path.read) end memoize def name bonuses = [bonus_a, bonus_b].compact if bonuses.empty? base_name else "#{base_name} (#{bonuses.join(", ")})" end end memoize def base_name [ data["Description"]["Name"], data["Description"]["UIName"], ].compact.last.gsub(" +", "+") end memoize def bonus_a data["BonusValueA"] == "" ? nil : data["BonusValueA"].gsub(/[a-z]\K\./, "") end memoize def bonus_b data["BonusValueB"] == "" ? nil : data["BonusValueB"].gsub(/[a-z]\K\./, "") end def category @data["Category"] end def tonnage @data["Tonnage"] end def damage shots * base_damage end def base_damage @data["Damage"] end def shots @data["ShotsWhenFired"] end def heat @data["HeatGenerated"] end def heat_tonnage heat / 3.0 end def ammo_tonnage # TODO 0 end def total_tonnage tonnage + heat_tonnage + ammo_tonnage end def ammo_category @data["ammoCategoryID"] end def purchasable? @data["Description"]["Purchasable"] end
end class BattleTechGame extend Memoist def initialize(game_root, *dlc_roots) @game_root = Pathname(game_root) @dlc_roots = dlc_roots.map{|path| Pathname(path)} end memoize def data_root @game_root + "BattleTech_Data/StreamingAssets/data" end memoize def weapon_files [data_root, *@dlc_roots] .flat_map{|root| root.glob("weapon/*.json")} .select{|n| n.basename.to_s != "WeaponTemplate.json"} end memoize def weapons weapon_files.map{|path| Weapon.new(path)} end def print_weapons_report weapons .select{|w| w.category != "Melee" and w.name != "AI Laser"} .sort_by{|w| [w.damage.to_f / w.total_tonnage, w.damage, w.name] } .reverse .each{|w| puts "* #{w.name}: #{w.damage} / #{w.total_tonnage.round(2)}" } end
end game = BattleTechGame.new(*ARGV)
game.print_weapons_report

To run the scirpt I use ./weapons /path/to/battletech /path/to/heavymetal

Result

Here's the result. As the script still doesn't count ammo, it overvalues ammo using weapons. It's also quite confused by any weapon that has special effects like all those TAGs and NARCs and COILs on the end of the list. It might be best to just exclude them the analysis.

Items.Title: 5 个高级 PHP 技巧，只有专业的 PHP 开发人员才会遵循 | 5 Advanced PHP Tricks That Only Expert PHP Developers Follow
Items.Description:
介绍
它提供了增强的性能、更好的安全性和帮助开发人员更快地创建 Web 应用程序的工具。 从那时起，它在许多项目中受到欢迎。 该语言提供了增强的性能、更好的安全性以及帮助开发人员更快地创建 Web 应用程序的工具。

它旨在使用户可以轻松添加和自定义功能以满足他们的需求。 高级 PHP 主要针对企业级使用。 然而，它是可扩展的、健壮的，并且可以在多个平台上使用。 高级 PHP 的重要性
高级 PHP 是一个总称，指代各种编程语言、框架、工具和实践。 它最初是在 2004 年创造的，当时一群开发人员开始使用该术语作为描述他们在 PHP 开发中最新发展的包罗万象的方式。

高级 PHP 包括以下功能：

  • 静态打字
  • 面向对象编程 (OOP)
  • 作曲家
  • 依赖注入模式 (DI)
  • 领域特定语言 (DSL) - 为特定应用程序领域编写的一组代码，旨在简洁明了。

高级 PHP 允许开发人员利用封装、面向对象编程和 OOP。 多年来，它添加了许多新功能。 随着越来越多的网站建立在高级 PHP 框架上，越来越多的开发人员开始学习它。 这也导致了使用高级 PHP 框架创建的大量项目。 高级 PHP 是当今网站开发中最关键的技术之一，因为开发人员使用它来构建各种功能，如 Web 应用程序、数字代理、电子商务商店等。
只有专家开发人员才能遵循的五个高级 PHP 技巧

高级 PHP 是互联网上最流行的编程语言，它已经经历了多次迭代。 它很受欢迎，因为许多行业和公司都在使用它。 高级 PHP 的流行主要是由于其稳定性、易用性和速度。 高级 PHP 还可以扩展以在其他编程语言（例如 Python 或 Ruby）中创建新功能。

高级 PHP 不仅使您的代码更易于阅读，而且还提高了您网站的性能。

以下是只有专业开发人员在学习 PHP 时才会遵循的五个 PHP 技巧：

  • 学习面向对象编程
  • 编写自文档化的代码
  • 学习使用 PHP 框架
  • 获取指定 URL 或文件的大小
  • 构建自己的框架

大多数新的 PHP 程序员学习如何在没有任何结构或组织的情况下编写程序代码。 当然，程序化代码并没有错，但是随着你的成长，你会发现如果你想要做一个更好的网站，你必须更好地划分和组织你的代码。 然后，开发人员可以将代码分解为类和对象调用，这就是面向对象编程的用武之地。因此，在开始实际项目之前，请确保您彻底了解 OOP。

无论他们使用 PHP 还是其他语言来编写服务器脚本，这都是对每个开发人员的最佳提示。 不幸的是，初学者可能会忘记变量名、类名，有时甚至是整个部分代码。 正因为如此，始终建议使用适当命名的变量、函数和易于记忆的类来编写自脚本代码。 添加注释还可以帮助您区分代码中的不同部分。

开发人员可以更直接地完成很多工作，并且在使用框架时开发过程更加高效和有效。 开发社区认为 Laravel 是当今最好的 PHP 框架之一。 因此建议尝试 Laravel 或其他框架。

使用函数filesize()，您可以找出文件的大小。 这是一个有益的功能，因为它可以执行许多不同的任务。 它允许我们在 PHP 中有多个输出。 通过使用 echo 可以获取指定 URL 或文件的大小。 为了简化你的工作，记得使用"echo filesize("test.txt");" 必要时。

无论是学习还是开发，一旦你学会了如何使用一个框架，就构建一个属于你自己的 PHP 框架。 模仿这些框架如何工作的过程对于开发人员来说是一个很好的实践程序。 此外，在构建框架时，您将遇到各种新的高级 PHP 方法。 如果您尝试过它们会有所帮助。

结论

它已被用于构建网站、Web 应用程序和软件系统。 公司选择在其项目中使用 PHP 的原因有很多。 原因之一是它易于学习，并且具有许多开箱即用的功能。 它还允许开发人员在编码过程中具有很大的灵活性，因为他们可以轻松地使用不同的框架和环境。 另一个原因是它的受欢迎程度——PHP 吸引了喜欢自己使用代码的开发人员以及需要软件开发人员或系统管理员帮助的开发人员。

然而，PHP 如此古老而出名，它为您提供了一个庞大的开发者社区，通常可以帮助您完成您的旅程。 如果你想学习 PHP 并开始你的职业生涯，除了课程之外，社区也是一个巨大的帮助。



原文:

Introduction
Advanced PHP is the latest version of PHP available under the license GPL. It offers enhanced performance, better security, and tools that help developers create web applications faster. Advanced PHP was released on October 21, 2018, and Sebastian Bergmann has spearheaded its development. Since then, it has gained popularity amongst many projects. The language offers enhanced performance, better security, and tools that help developers create web apps faster.

Advanced PHP is a modular software package that takes an array of technologies and combines them into one. It is designed to make it easy for users to add and customise functions to suit their needs. Advanced PHP is primarily aimed at enterprise-level usage. However, it is scalable, robust, and used on multiple platforms. The PHP framework has been around since the late 1990s, but it was not until the release of version 5 in December 2006 that it became popular with developers and administrators alike.
Importance of Advanced PHP
Advanced PHP is an umbrella term that refers to various programming languages, frameworks, tools, and practices. It was first coined in 2004 when a group of developers started using the term as an all-encompassing way of describing their latest developments in PHP development.

Advanced PHP includes features such as:

  • Static Typing
  • Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
  • Composer
  • Dependency Injection Pattern (DI)
  • A domain-specific language (DSL) - A set of code written for one particular application domain, designed for clarity and brevity.

Advanced PHP allows developers to take advantage of encapsulation, object-oriented programming, and OOP. A lot of new features have been added to it over the years. As more and more websites are built on the advanced PHP framework, more and more developers have started learning it. This has also led to a wide range of projects created using advanced PHP frameworks. Advanced PHP is one of the most critical technologies in website development today as developers use it to build various features like web apps, digital agencies, e-commerce stores, etc.
Five Advanced PHP Tricks that Only Expert Developers Follow

Advanced PHP is the most popular programming language used on the internet, and It has been through many iterations. It's popular because many industries and companies are using it. The popularity of advanced PHP is due primarily to its stability, ease of use, and speed. Advanced PHP can also be extended to create new features in other programming languages such as Python or Ruby.

PHP is easy to learn and has many developers supporting it. Advanced PHP not only makes your code easier to read but also accelerates your website's performance.

Here are the five PHP tricks that only expert developers follow when they learn PHP –

  • Learn object-oriented programming
  • Write self-documented code
  • Learn to use a PHP framework
  • Get the size of the specified URL or file
  • Build your own framework

1. Learn object-oriented programming

Most new PHP programmers learn how to write procedural code without any structure or organisation. Of course, there is nothing wrong with procedural code, but as you grow, you will discover that you have to divide and organise your code better if you want to make a better website. Then, the developer can break down the code into classes and object calls, where object-oriented programming comes in. So, before you begin work on an actual project, make sure you thoroughly understand OOPs.

2. Write self-documented code

Whether they use PHP or another language for server scripting, this is the best tip for every developer. Unfortunately, beginners can forget variable names, classes names, and sometimes the entire section code. Because of this, it is always recommended to write self-scripting code with appropriately named variables, functions, and classes that you can remember easily. Adding comments can also help you distinguish the different sections in the code.

3. Learn to use a PHP framework

Developers get a lot of work done more straightforward, and the development process is more efficient and effective when they use frameworks. The development community considers Laravel one of the best PHP frameworks available today. It is therefore advisable to give Laravel or other frameworks a try.

4. Get the size of the specified URL or file

Using the function filesize(), you can find out the size of a file. It is a beneficial function because it can perform many different tasks. The echo command lets us create a string of text and repeat it back while adding some additional information at the beginning or end of the string. It allows us to have multiple outputs in our PHP. It is possible to get the size of a specified URL or File by using it with echo. To simplify your work, remember to use "echo filesize("test.txt");" wherever necessary.

5. Build your own framework

Whether it is just for learning or development, build a PHP framework of your own once you have learned how to use a framework. The process of mimicking how these frameworks work is a great practice routine for developers. Additionally, you will encounter a variety of new advanced PHP methods when building your framework. It would help if you tried them.

Conclusion

PHP has been around for over 20 years and is one of the most popular open-source programming languages. It has been used to build websites, web applications, and software systems. There are many reasons why companies choose to use PHP in their projects. One reason is that it's easy to learn, and it comes with a lot of features out-of-the-box. It also allows developers a lot of flexibility in the coding process because they can easily use different frameworks and environments. The other reason is its popularity - PHP appeals to developers who prefer working with code on their own and those who need assistance from software developers or system administrators.

However, PHP being so old and famous, provides you with a massive community of developers that often helps you out on your journey. If you want to learn PHP and start your career, the community is also an enormous help besides the courses.

Hello Сommunity and PowerPC enthusiasts around the world! The AlmaLinux Foundation is proud to announce that the general availability of AlmaLinux OS 8.5 Stable for ppc64le is live.

Images and Release Notes

You can now grab the image and take a look at the Release Notes for the changelog and installation instructions.

Containers and Cloud Too

All you Container and cloud fans will be happy too. PowerPC 8.5 container images are already available on Docker Hub and should be updated to the latest stable images real soon and we'll providing a generic cloud (cloud-init) image in the repository as well.

Thanks

AlmaLinux would like to express our sincere gratitude to those that lent an extra hand to make this release possible. This release would not have been possible without the tremendous support of both the Oregon State University Open Source Lab as well as researchers from CERN and dozens of other PowerPC community members, who have provided resources, testing, feedback and more to help us build and fine tune every aspect of this release. We proud to be a part of, and work with such an amazing community of people across the globe.

How to help

As always, your contributions, feedback and bug reports help make AlmaLinux great. Please, report any bugs you may see on the Bug Tracker. Join the party on the AlmaLinux Community Chat and ask any question you have on our AlmaLinux Community Forum and on Reddit. Catch us on Twitter too.

Looking forward to move releases soon!

    原文:

    Algunos de los factores más comunes y que más impactan el “performance” de una aplicación están relacionados con las consultas en base de datos muy grandes, por lo tanto, entender cómo detectar y solucionar estos problemas es de gran relevancia en cualquier equipo de desarrollo backend.

    Recientemente en Ayenda se detectó que una consulta a una tabla de nuestra base de datos estaba tardando demasiado tiempo en ser procesada, la consulta se hacía a una tabla con millones de registros y tardaba alrededor de 7.8 segundos en ser procesada en un entorno de desarrollo local.

    Existen diferentes estrategias para abordar la solución a este problema, una de ellas y la implementada en este caso, es el uso de índices en la base de datos. Por tal motivo, desde el equipo de Tech en Ayenda queremos compartirles este blog post donde hablaremos de la definición, creación y aplicación de algunos tipos de índices en PostgreSQL bajo una aplicación construida con Ruby on Rails.

    Temas tratados en el blog:

    • Indexación en bases de datos.
    • Definición de un índice en una base de datos.
    • Índice B-Tree
    • Índice GIN (Generalized Inverted Indexes).
    • Creación de índices en Ruby on Rails.
    • Uso del índice B-Tree.
    • Creación y uso de índice GIN en Ruby on Rails
    • Índices multicolumna
    • Combinando multiples índices

    Indexación en bases de datos.

    Supongamos que queremos encontrar un usuario con un número de identificación específico en nuestra base de datos. Utilizando ActiveRecord podríamos ejecutar un query como el siguiente.

    User.where(id_number: 123456789)

    Cuando ejecutamos el query anterior, por lo general, PostgreSQL hará un escaneo secuencial para leer todas las filas de la tabla User buscando algún registro donde la columna id_number sea igual al número 123456789. Esto puede convertirse en un problema cuando la consulta se hace en una tabla que contiene millones de datos, debido a que el sistema tiene que buscar en toda la tabla para encontrar un registro dado, lo que puede impactar fuertemente en el perfomance de la base de datos. Sin embargo, al agregar un índice a la columna de id_number, PostgreSQL podrá realizar el query utilizando el índice creado para hacer un escaneo de la tabla mucho más eficiente.

    A diferencia de un escaneo secuencial, un escaneo por índice es mucho más rápido, ya que en lugar de hacer una consulta donde el tiempo de ejecución está relacionado linealmente con la cantidad de datos ‘O(n)’, se hará un consulta con un performance logarítmico ‘O(log n)’.

    Definición de un índice en una base de datos (DB).

    Un índice, en palabras simples, es una estructura de datos que se define sobre una o varias columnas y permite ubicar de forma eficiente un registro determinado en una tabla. Una vez creado un índice, el sistema lo actualizará cuando se modifique la tabla y lo utilizará en consultas determinadas, por lo tanto, los índices aumentan la velocidad en las consultas solo para las instrucciones SELECT, UPDATE y DELETE, no en comandos de inserción CREATE. Adicionalmente un índice por definición debe ocupar un espacio en el disco duro, donde almacenará la información de su estructura de datos. Existen diferentes tipos de índices y cada uno utiliza un algoritmo particular que se adapta mejor al tipo de dato y al tipo de consulta. Actualmente PostgreSQL soporta varios tipos de índices, algunos de ellos son: B-tree, Hash, GiST y GIN.

    Índice B-Tree

    El índice B-Tree es el más utilizado debido a su versatilidad y además es el índice creado por defecto en PostgreSQL cuando no se especifica el tipo. B-Tree es un tipo especial de estructura de datos de tipo árbol autobalanceado en el que cada nodo puede contener más de una clave y más de dos nodos hijos. Este tipo de índice es utilizado comúnmente en datos de tipo numérico, fecha/hora y de tipo carácter (con algunas limitaciones). La siguiente imagen muestra la estructura de datos para un índice B-Tree.

    Por lo general PostgreSQL considerará utilizar el índice B-Tree siempre que una columna indexada esté involucrada en una consulta que utilice alguno de los siguientes operadores: (<, <=, =, >=, >), BETWEEN, IN y condiciones como IS NULL o IS NOT NULL. PostgreSQL también considera utilizar este tipo de índice para consultas que involucran operadores de patrones de coincidencia como LIKE y ~ siempre y cuando el patrón sea una constante y esté en el comienzo de la cadena, por ejemplo, LIKE 'foo%' o ~ '^ foo', pero nunca para LIKE '%foo'. Por último también es posible utilizarlo con los operadores ILIKE y ~*, pero solo si el patrón comienza con caracteres no alfabéticos, es decir, caracteres que no son afectados por conversión de mayúsculas o minúsculas.

    Índice GIN (Generalized Inverted Indexes).

    El índice GIN es utilizado en tipos de datos que almacenan información estructurada como arrays, jsonb o hstore, adicionalmente se utiliza con frecuencia en implementaciones para optimizar búsquedas de texto. Este índice almacena un conjunto de pares (clave, lista), donde la lista es un conjunto de IDs de las filas donde aparece la clave. Un ID de una lista puede aparecer en otras listas, ya que un elemento puede contener más de una clave. Cada clave se almacena sólo una vez, por lo que el índice GIN es muy compacto para los casos en que la misma clave aparece muchas veces en los registros. Un ejemplo de la estructura de datos para un índice GIN se muestra a continuación.

    Este tipo de índices admite diferentes estrategias de indexación para cada tipo de dato, por ende, los operadores con los que se puede usar este índice varían según la estrategia de indexación, por ejemplo, la implementación de GIN por defecto en PostgreSQL para un array admite consultas con los siguientes operadores (<@, @>, =, &&). Ejemplos:

    • [‘a’, ‘b’, ‘c’] @> [‘a’, ‘b’, ‘b’, ‘c’] evalúa si [‘a’, ‘b’, ‘c’] contiene a [‘a’, ‘b’, ‘b’, ‘c’] (verdadero)
    • [1, 1, 4] <@ [4, 3, 2, 1] evalúa si [1, 1, 4] es contenido por [4, 3, 2, 1] (verdadero)
    • [1, 2] && [2, 3] evalúa si los dos array se superponen (verdadero)

    Creación de índices en Ruby on Rails.

    Ahora bien, Ruby on Rails nos brinda la capacidad gestionar la creación de índices mediante migraciones con ActiveRecord, por ejemplo, con la siguiente instrucción podemos crear la tabla IntegerTable con dos columnas de tipo entero (integer_a, integer_b) y con un índice para la columna integer_b.

    rails g model IntegerTable integer_a:integer integer_b:integer:index

    La instrucción anterior genera la migración que se muestra a continuación, donde podemos observar el método add_index encargado de generar el índice a la tabla y columna correspondiente. Si no se especifica el tipo de índice se crea por default un índice tipo B-Tree.

    class CreateIntegerTables < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.0]
  def change
    create_table :integer_tables do |t|
      t.integer :integer_a
      t.integer :integer_b

      t.timestamps
    end
    add_index :integer_tables, :integer_b
  end
end

    Uso del índice B-Tree.

    Para analizar el uso de los índices B-Tree en diferentes tipos de datos utilizaremos algunas tablas y columnas con información creada previamente. Cada tabla tiene dos columnas del mismo tipo y con la misma información, sin embargo una de las columnas estará indexada con B-tree y la otra no. En los ejemplos utilizados a continuación contamos con alrededor de un millón de registros aleatorios para cada tabla. Para observar los resultados utilizaremos el IDE, DBeaver Community Edition (https://dbeaver.io/) y el comando EXPLAIN ANALYZE en cada query para conocer los detalles de las consultas ejecutadas. En el siguiente repositorio se encuentra el proyecto utilizado para todas la consultas mostradas en este blog: (https://github.com/julianpz21/database_indexes)

    Índice B-Tree en columnas tipo números.

    Analicemos el comportamiento de dos consultas realizadas a la tabla Integer_tables, la primera consulta se realiza sobre la columna sin indexar (integer_a) y la segunda consulta sobre la columna indexada mediante B-tree (integer_b). En las siguientes figuras podemos observar las diferencias entre el tiempo de ejecución y el tipo de query de cada una de las consultas.

    La consulta sobre la columna integer_a utiliza un Parallel Seq Scan y se demora en ejecutarse alrededor de 39.399 ms.

    La consulta sobre la columna integer_b utiliza un Index Scan y se demora en ejecutarse alrededor de 0.054 ms, algo así como 730 veces más rápido.

    Índice B-Tree en columnas tipo fecha.

    Igual que el ejemplo anterior, tenemos una tabla llamada date_tables con dos columnas (date_a, date_b), donde la última es indexada mediante B-tree. De nuevo, en las siguientes figuras podemos observar las diferencias entre el tiempo de ejecución y el tipo de query de cada una de las consultas.

    La consulta sobre la columna date_a utiliza un Parallel Seq Scan y tarda en ejecutarse alrededor de 43.293 ms.

    La consulta sobre la columna date_b utiliza un Index Scan y tarda en ejecutarse alrededor de 0.054 ms, algo así como 800 veces más rápido.

    Índice B-Tree en columnas tipo strings.

    Para analizar este escenario contamos con una tabla llamada string_tables que tiene dos columnas (string_a, string_b), donde la última es indexada mediante B-tree. De nuevo, en las siguientes figuras podemos observar las diferencias entre el tiempo de ejecución y el tipo de query de cada una de las consultas.

    La consulta sobre la columna string_a utiliza un Parallel Seq Scan y tarda en ejecutarse alrededor de 52.202 ms.

    La consulta sobre la columna string_b utiliza un Index Scan y tarda en ejecutarse alrededor de 0.062 ms, algo así como 841 veces más rápido.

    Índice B-tree y el operador LIKE e ILIKE

    Ahora bien, las consultas en columnas que tienen caracteres son algo especiales debido a que podemos utilizar en ellas operadores de patrones de coincidencia como LIKE o ILIKE, sin embargo se debe tener especial cuidado al utilizarlos. Para entender esto, haremos algunos ejemplos que nos mostrarán cuando la base de datos utiliza o no un Index Scan aun cuando la consulta se realiza sobre la columna indexada string_b.

    Caso LIKE: PostgreSQL utilizará el índice B-tree (Index Scan) cuando una wildcard como ‘%’ NO está definida en el comienzo de la cadena, veamos un ejemplo:

    Se realiza un Index Scan cuando la wildcard ‘%’ No está al comienzo de la cadena de caracteres.

    En el caso contrario, PostgreSQL No utilizará el índice cuando una wildcard como ‘%’ está definida al comienzo de la cadena.

    Observemos en el siguiente ejemplo como se realiza un Parallel Seq Scan cuando la wildcard ‘%’ está al comienzo de la cadena de caracteres. PostgreSQL no utiliza el índice en este tipo de query.

    Caso ILIKE: En este tipo de consultas PostgreSQL utilizará el índice solo si el patrón comienza con caracteres no alfabéticos, es decir, caracteres que no se ven afectados por la conversión de mayúsculas o minúsculas.

    Se realiza un Index Scan con el operador ILIKE cuando el patrón de búsqueda comienza con un carácter no alfabético, esto se muestra en la siguiente imagen donde el primer carácter del string en el query comienza con el número 1.

    En caso contrario, PostgreSQL no utilizará el índice B-tree para consultas con el operador ILIKE. En la siguiente imagen vemos que se realiza un Parallel Seq Scan con el operador ILIKE cuando el patrón de búsqueda comienza con un carácter alfabético.

    Creación y uso de índice GIN en Ruby on Rails

    El índice GIN puede ser utilizado en columnas de tipo arrays, jsonb, hstore y texto. Un ejemplo que muestra creación de este tipo de índice en una columna de tipo array de enteros se muestra a continuación, en donde se especifica en la migración utilizar un índice GIN solo para la columna array_b.

    class CreateArrayTables < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.0]
  def change
    create_table :array_tables do |t|
      t.integer :array_a, array: true
      t.integer :array_b, array: true

      t.timestamps
    end
    add_index :array_tables, :array_b, using: 'gin'
  end
end

    En las siguiente figuras podemos observar y comparar la diferencia en el tiempo de ejecución entre una consulta que se realiza sobre una columna sin indexar y otra que se realiza sobre una columna indexada con GIN. La consulta sobre la columna indexada array_b es más eficiente.

    Se utiliza Index Scan cuando la consulta se hace sobre la columna con índice GIN (array_b).

    Índices multicolumna

    Un índice puede ser definido en más de una columna. PostgreSQL permite indexar hasta un máximo de 32 columnas, pero únicamente para índices tipo B-tree, Gist, Gin y Brin. Este tipo de índices es útil cuando tenemos consultas que involucran el operador AND entre varias columnas. Un índice multicolumna se puede crear en Ruby on Rails de la siguiente manera

    class CreateMulticolumnIndexTables < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.0]
  def change
    create_table :multicolumn_index_tables do |t|
      t.integer :integer_a
      t.integer :integer_b

      t.timestamps
    end
    add_index :multicolumn_index_tables, %i[integer_a integer_b]
  end
end

    PostgreSQL utiliza el índice multicolumna creado cuando en una consulta intervienen las dos columnas indexadas con el operador AND. Observemos como el resultados del Explain Analyze nos muestra el uso de Index Scan.

    El índice multicolumna también es utilizado cuando se hace una consulta solo para la columna integer_a, la cual corresponde a la columna definida más a la izquierda en la creación del índice.

    Sin embargo, PostgreSQL NO utiliza el índice multicolumna para las consultadas que involucran el operador OR o solamente una columna que corresponda a la definida más a la derecha en la creación del índice, veamos los siguientes ejemplo para observar el comportamiento.

    NO se realiza un Index Scan en un índice multicolumna para las consultas con el operador OR.

    NO se utiliza el índice multicolumna cuando en la consulta solo interviene la columna definida más a la derecha en el índice. Recordemos que el orden de las columnas en la creación del indice fue [integer_a integer_b]

    Combinando multiples índices

    PostgreSQL tiene la capacidad de solucionar el inconveniente anterior combinando múltiples índices definidos individualmente (incluidos múltiples usos del mismo índice). De esta forma podemos ejecutar consultas con múltiples condiciones AND y OR a través del uso de los índices individuales de cada columna.

    Para ejemplificar este comportamiento, crearemos índices individualmente para cada columna en una migración de la siguiente manera.

    class CreateCombiningIndexTables < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.0]
  def change
    create_table :combining_index_tables do |t|
      t.integer :integer_a
      t.integer :integer_b

      t.timestamps
    end
    add_index :combining_index_tables, :integer_a
    add_index :combining_index_tables, :integer_b
  end
end

    Luego de correr la migración anterior, podemos realizar una consulta con varias columnas indexadas individualmente, adicionalmente podemos utilizar el operador OR y PostgreSQL utilizará los índices creados en la consulta.

    Como se muestra a continuación, en la consulta se utilizan los índices individuales de cada columna, a esto se le llama combinar múltiples índices.

    One more thing! Cómo agregar índices a una tabla de una base de datos en producción?

    Como se mencionó anteriormente, PostgreSQL y muchos otros RDBMS bloquean el acceso de escritura en la tabla mientras un índice es creado. Por supuesto, esto es inaceptable cuando nuestro proyecto está en producción y no podemos permitir un tiempo de inactividad considerable mientras se crea el índice. Hay que tener en cuenta que mientras más grande sea la tabla a indexar, más tiempo se tardara nuestro índice en ser creado.
    Para sortear este problema, PostgreSQL nos ofrece una herramienta que construirá el índice sin tomar ningún bloqueo que impida las inserciones, actualizaciones o eliminaciones simultáneas en la tabla. Para esto se debe pasar en la creación del índice el siguiente parámetro algorithm: :concurrently.

    El parámetro algorithm: :concurrently permite crear el índice sin bloquear la tabla mientras este es creado. Note que se debe agregar disable_ddl_transaction! para permitir que la migración no se ejecute por defecto dentro de una transacción.

    class AddIndexToSimpleTable < ActiveRecord::Migration[7.0]
  disable_ddl_transaction!

  def change
    add_index :simple_tables, :integer_a, algorithm: :concurrently
  end
end

    Solución al problema planteado al principio del blog:

    Se decide crear un índice multicolumna B-Tree en la tabla, esta migración tarda alrededor de 26 segundos en ser completada en el entorno de desarrollo local. La misma consulta realizada luego de la creación de los índices tardó alrededor de 0.15 ms en ser ejecutad, mucho más veloz que los 7.8 segundos que se tardaba antes de crear el índice.

    Algunos consejos finales:

    Es complejo formular un procedimiento general para determinar qué índices crear, pero los siguientes son algunos consejos que le permitirán tomar una mejor decisión.

    • Siempre analiza las consultas con el comando EXPLAIN ANALYZE, de esta manera puedes comprender que hacer correctamente sin adivinar a ciegas qué sucederá si se agrega o elimina un índice.
    • El índice predeterminado de PostgreSQL es el índice B-Tree.
    • Utilice datos reales para la experimentación cuando va a crear y probar un índice. Es decir, que la tabla en el entorno local donde realizará las pruebas del comportamiento del índice tenga un numero de registros similar al entorno de producción.
    • Es recomendable crear índices solo sobre las consultas ejecutadas con más frecuencia y que son más costosas a nivel de performance. Evite crear un índice para satisfacer una consulta específica que no impacte el rendimiento de su aplicación.
    • Tómese el tiempo necesario para abordar la solución, itere si es necesario. Es mejor analizar cuál índice es el apropiado para la consulta y descartar los índices innecesarios.
    • No hay un límite específico en el número de índices en una tabla; sin embargo, se recomienda crear la menor cantidad de índices que satisfagan su carga de trabajo.

Items.Title: Web 浏览器如何工作 - 解析 CSS（第 4 部分，附插图）⏳🌐 | How web browsers work - parsing the CSS (part 4, with illustrations)⏳🌐
Items.Description:
在 HTML 被解析之后，是时候parse the CSS （在外部 CSS 文件和样式元素中找到）并构建CSSOM tree （CSS 对象模型）了。

当浏览器遇到 CSS 样式表时，无论是外部的还是嵌入的，它都需要将文本解析为可用于设置布局样式的内容。 浏览器转向的数据结构
就像从我们的 HTML 中构建 DOM 一样，从 CSS 中构建 CSSOM 被认为是一个渲染阻塞过程。

代币化和构建 CSSOM

与 HTML 解析类似，CSS 解析从标记化开始。 浏览器会做一些称为selector machting的事情，这意味着每组样式都将与页面上的所有节点（元素）进行匹配。

浏览器从适用于节点的最通用规则开始（例如：如果节点是 body 元素的子节点，则所有 body 样式都由该节点继承），然后通过应用更具体的规则递归地细化计算的样式。 这就是为什么我们说样式规则是cascading的。

想象一下我们有下面的 CSS：

 body { font-size : 16px ; color : white ; h1 { font-size : 32px ; } section { color : tomato ; } .mainTitle { margin-left : 5px } div { font-size : 20px ; } div p { font-size : 8px ; color : yellow ; } }

这段代码的 CSSOM 看起来像这样：

请注意，在上面的模式中，嵌套元素既有inherited styles （从父级 - 例如： h1从body继承其颜色， section从body继承其 font-size）和their own styles （可以覆盖继承的规则是否来自父节点 - 例如： p覆盖从div继承的颜色和字体大小，并且mainTitle不会从父节点获取其左边距）。

由于我们的 CSS 可以有多个源，并且它们可以包含适用于同一节点的规则，因此浏览器必须决定最终应用哪个规则。 这就是specificity发挥作用的时候，如果您想了解更多关于它的信息，您可以访问此页面。

想象一下，您在机场，正在寻找您的朋友约翰。 如果你想通过喊他的名字来找到他，你可以喊“约翰”。 很有可能不止一个约翰会同时在机场，所以他们可能都会做出回应。 更好的方法是用他的全名称呼你的朋友，这样当你喊“John Doe”时，你就有更好的机会找到他，因为“John Doe”比“John”更具体。

同样，假设我们有这个元素：

 <p> <a href= "https://dev.to/" > This is just a link! </a> </p>

以及这些 CSS 样式：

 a { color : red ; } p a { color : blue ; }

您认为浏览器会应用哪个规则？ 答案是第二条规则，因为all anchor tags inside a paragraph所有锚标签都比all anchor tags选择器具有更多的特异性。 如果你想玩弄特异性，你可以使用这个特异性计算器。

重要的
如果是这种情况，它将应用 CSS 规则。

来源
直到 2016 年的 CSS 简史
MDN Web Docs MDN 网络文档



原文:

After the HTML has been parsed, it's time to parse the CSS (found in in external CSS files and in style elements) and build the CSSOM tree (CSS Object Model).

When the browser encounters a CSS stylesheet, be it external or embeded, it needs to parse the text into something it can use for styling the layouts. The data structure that the browser turns the
CSS into is called the CSSOM. The DOM and the CSSOM follow similar concepts, in the sense that they are both trees, but they are different data structures. Just like building the DOM out of our HTML, building the CSSOM out of CSS is considered a render-blocking process.

Tokenization & building the CSSOM

Similar to HTML parsing, CSS parsing starts with tokenization. The CSS parser takes the bytes and converts them into characters, then tokens, then nodes and finally they are linked into the CSSOM. The browser does something called selector machting which means that each set of styles will be matched against all nodes (elements) on the page.

The browser starts with the most general rule applicable to a node (e.g: if a node it's the child of the body element, then all body styles are inherited by that node) and then recursively refines the computed styles by applying more specific rules. This is why we say that the style rules are cascading.

Imagine we have the CSS below:

body { font-size: 16px; color: white; h1 { font-size: 32px;
} section { color: tomato;
} .mainTitle { margin-left: 5px
} div { font-size: 20px;
} div p { font-size: 8px; color: yellow;
}
}

The CSSOM for this code would look something like this:

Note that in the schema above, the nested elements have both inherited styles (from the parent - e.g: h1 inherits its color from the body and the section inherits its font-size from the body) and their own styles (which can overwrite rules inherited from the parent or not - e.g: p overwrites both the color and the font-size inherited from the div and mainTitle doesn't get its left margin from a parent node).

Since we can have multiple sources for our CSS and they can contain rules that apply to the same node, the browser must decide which rule will apply in the end. That's when specificity comes into play and if you want to read more about it, you can visit this page.

Imagine you're in the airport and you're looking for your friend John. If you want to find him by calling out his name, you could call for "John". Chances are that more than one John will be in the airport at the same time, so they might all respond. A better approach would be to call your friend using his full name, so that when you shout "John Doe", you'll have better chances to finding him, since "John Doe" is more specific than just "John".

On the same note, let's say we have this element:

<p> <a href="https://dev.to/">This is just a link!</a>
</p>

and these CSS styles:

a { color: red;
} p a { color: blue;
}

Which rule do you think will the browser apply? The answer is the second rules, since all anchor tags inside a paragraph selector combination has more specificity than just all anchor tags selector. If you want to play around with specificity, you can use this Specificity calculator.

IMPORTANT
CSS rules are read from right to left, meaning that if we have something like: section p { color: blue; }, the browser will first look for all p tags on the page and then it will look if any of those p tags have a section tag as a parent. If that's the case, it will apply the CSS rule.

Sources
A brief history of CSS until 2016
MDN Web Docs
